name: Dominion Energy, Inc.

profile.description: Dominion Energy, Inc. provides regulated electric and natural gas utility services to approximately 4.5 million customers in Virginia, North Carolina, and South Carolina. The company operates the world's largest data center market in Northern Virginia with 51 GW of contracted data center capacity. In May 2026, Dominion agreed to a $67 billion all-stock merger with NextEra Energy to create the nation's largest regulated electric utility. Founded in 1983, headquartered in Richmond, Virginia.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (1/3): Dominion executed a major strategic pivot in 2020 by selling its gas transmission and storage business to Berkshire Hathaway for $9.7B and has since repositioned around regulated electric utility operations and data center demand [1][2]. The $11.4B Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind project is 75% complete with 9 turbines installed as of Q1 2026, representing the largest offshore wind installation in the U.S. [1]. However, these are infrastructure buildouts rather than product innovations or business model transformations — the core business remains generating and distributing electricity. AI Deployment (0/2): No evidence of AI-powered products shipping to customers or specific AI-driven cost savings quantified. Grid management and predictive maintenance tools are likely deployed but no measurable results have been publicly reported [2]. Market Share (2/2): Northern Virginia hosts the world's largest data center market — larger than the next four markets combined — and Dominion's contracted data center capacity has tripled to 51 GW since July 2023, with 10.4 GW under executed Electric Service Agreements [1][3]. Clear category leadership in the highest-growth utility demand segment. Product Pipeline (1/2): Small modular nuclear reactor development, 12,000 MW of new solar capacity, pumped storage hydroelectric, and battery storage facilities provide visible pipeline with the $65B five-year capital plan (2026-2030) [2][3]. SMR timelines remain uncertain. R&D Intensity (1/1): The $65B five-year capital plan is among the largest in the utility sector, and the offshore wind investment of $11.4B demonstrates capital commitment well above utility industry norms [1].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): Dominion's purpose centers on delivering 'reliable, affordable, and increasingly clean energy' — a clear articulation of what the company does and for whom, but not a transformative vision [2]. CEO Bob Blue has described three simultaneous transitions reshaping the industry — decarbonization, datafication/electrification, and grid resiliency — which adds strategic depth but remains operational rather than idealistic. The mission is interchangeable with any major regulated utility. Community Impact (1/2): Dominion's philanthropic programs include a $46.7M ESG budget with environmental grants (Hollywood Cemetery urban tree-planting), the 'Solar for Students' renewable energy education initiative, and Virginia ArtStars arts education grants [4]. The NextEra merger agreement includes $2.25B in customer bill credits. These are genuine but standard for a $61B market cap utility. Employee Retention (1/2): Dominion's OSHA recordable incident rate of 0.42 is 78% below the industry average of 1.90, signaling a genuinely strong safety culture that suggests employee welfare is prioritized [1]. CEO Blue's collaborative leadership style since 2020 indicates organizational stability [2]. No specific turnover or tenure data is publicly available. Insider Buying (1/2): CEO Blue's compensation is substantially equity-linked, aligning interests with shareholders, but no meaningful net insider buying beyond routine compensation was identified. The pending NextEra merger may constrain insider transactions due to trading restrictions.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. Q1 2026 revenue of $5.02B surged 23% YoY driven by data center demand and fuel cost recovery, with operating margins of 29% consistent across five quarters [1][3]. Full-year 2026 operating EPS guidance of $3.45-$3.69 implies 5-7% long-term growth [1]. D/E of 155% is elevated but typical for capital-intensive regulated utilities (metric score 0.63). ROE of 9.79% is below the 15% target (score 0.65). Revenue growth YoY of 17.1% exceeds target (score 1.0). Revenue growth 5yr CAGR of 1.6% is weak due to the 2021 revenue dip following the gas divestiture (score 0.44). Forward P/E of 19.33 is near the 20 target (score 1.0). Profit margin of 16.93% is below the 20% target (score 0.85). Payout ratio of 79.25% is high (score 0.35). Consecutive years paid of 34 is strong (score 1.0, 2x weight). Consecutive years of dividend growth is 0 after the 2020 cut (score 0.0). 5-year dividend growth CAGR of -3.19% is negative (score 0.0, 2x weight) [5][6]. Weighted sum 6.92 / max 12 = 0.577, infrastructure = round(5.77) = 6.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Dominion Energy's core business — physical electric generation, transmission, and distribution infrastructure serving 4.5 million customers — is structurally immune to AI disruption [1]. Power generation requires physical plants, turbines, transformers, and thousands of miles of transmission lines that no software can replace. The business is deeply regulated by state public utility commissions, creating additional structural barriers. Most critically, AI is a direct and powerful demand driver for Dominion: Northern Virginia hosts the world's largest data center market, and contracted capacity has tripled to 51 GW since July 2023, with commercial sales surging 8.4% in Q1 2026 driven primarily by data center expansion [1][3]. The AI compute buildout requires massive amounts of electricity, directly increasing Dominion's rate base and earnings power. Scoring 8 rather than 9 because Dominion is not itself building AI infrastructure — it is a beneficiary, not a creator, and execution risk in building generation capacity fast enough to meet demand is real. AI Opportunity (1/1): AI data center demand is generating quantifiable revenue growth — Q1 2026 revenue surged 23% YoY to $5.02B, driven in significant part by data center load growth [1][3]. The 51 GW pipeline and $65B five-year capital plan are directly tied to serving AI infrastructure demand.

infinite_game.summary: Dominion Energy under CEO Bob Blue demonstrates meaningful infinite game characteristics — a regulated utility inherently operates on multi-decade time horizons, and the company has made transformative strategic decisions including the 2020 gas transmission divestiture and the $67B NextEra merger. The $11.4B offshore wind bet and SMR exploration reflect long-term capability building. However, the merger itself raises questions about whether leadership believes Dominion can endure independently, and the broken dividend growth record signals short-term financial optimization over long-term shareholder trust.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Dominion's purpose of delivering 'reliable, affordable, and increasingly clean energy' is genuine and connects to the broader societal challenge of the energy transition [2]. CEO Blue has articulated three simultaneous industry transitions — decarbonization, datafication, and resiliency — and the company's strategic decisions trace back to this framework: divesting gas assets, building the largest U.S. offshore wind project, pursuing SMRs, and positioning to serve AI data center demand [2][3]. The $11.4B offshore wind investment demonstrates the purpose shapes capital allocation. However, the mission has not yet been tested through a crisis where leadership sacrificed short-term results to advance it, and the purpose statement itself is functional rather than visionary.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Dominion demonstrated meaningful courage in 2020 by selling gas transmission and storage to Berkshire Hathaway for $9.7B, voluntarily shrinking the company and cutting the dividend from ~$3.76 to ~$2.50 to refocus on regulated electric operations [2]. The $11.4B Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind project — the largest in the U.S. — represents a genuine long-term bet with $9.4B invested through Q1 2026 at a levelized cost of $84/MWh well below the $149/MWh prudency cap [1]. However, the 2020 dividend cut was driven more by financial necessity than strategic courage, and agreeing to the NextEra acquisition could be interpreted as ceding leadership rather than building independently for the long term.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Dominion's safety culture is exceptional — an OSHA recordable incident rate of 0.42 is 78% below the 1.90 industry average, indicating strong organizational discipline and employee welfare commitment [1]. CEO Blue emphasizes collaborative decision-making across 'all the different constituencies that influence everything we do' [2]. The 15,200-employee regulated utility workforce typically benefits from stable employment, strong benefits, and union representation. However, no specific data on voluntary turnover, employee engagement, or cultural practices was found, and the pending NextEra merger introduces workforce uncertainty.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2): Dominion has demonstrated transformative flexibility twice in recent years. First, the 2020 sale of gas transmission and storage assets to Berkshire Hathaway for $9.7B fundamentally reshaped the company from a diversified energy conglomerate to a focused regulated electric utility — including a painful ~34% dividend cut [2]. Second, the agreement to the $67B NextEra merger in May 2026 represents a willingness to sacrifice corporate independence to create the nation's largest regulated utility, better positioned to serve the energy transition and AI-driven demand [7]. The aggressive pivot toward data center infrastructure — contracting 51 GW from 16 GW in three years — shows speed and decisiveness in responding to structural industry shifts [1][3].

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Dominion's decision to merge with NextEra Energy — its largest and most capable rival — is a profound acknowledgment that the combined entity can better serve the energy transition than either company independently [7]. Rather than competing to 'beat' NextEra in the data center and renewable energy race, Dominion chose partnership, reflecting an infinite game recognition that the mission of delivering clean, reliable energy is bigger than any single company. CEO Blue's language focuses on serving customers and meeting demand rather than defeating competitors [2].

investment_thesis: Dominion Energy sits at the epicenter of the AI-driven electricity demand supercycle, operating the world's largest data center market in Northern Virginia with 51 GW of contracted capacity — tripling since mid-2023 [1][3]. Q1 2026 revenue surged 23% YoY to $5.02B with operating EPS of $0.95 beating consensus of $0.86, and the pending $67B merger with NextEra Energy will create the nation's largest regulated utility with enhanced scale for renewable energy and grid infrastructure investment [7]. At forward P/E of 19.33, dividend yield of 3.84%, and 5-7% long-term EPS growth guidance biased to the upper half through 2030, Dominion offers a compelling regulated utility profile uniquely levered to the AI infrastructure buildout [1].
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on execution risk and the broken dividend story. The NextEra merger requires 12-18 months of regulatory approvals across FERC, NRC, and three state commissions — any of which could impose costly conditions or block the transaction [7]. Dominion's dividend has 0 consecutive years of growth and a negative 5-year CAGR of -3.19% after the 2020 cut, with a 79.25% payout ratio limiting future increases, making it an impaired income holding relative to utility peers with 10+ year dividend growth streaks [5][6]. D/E of 155% combined with $65B in planned five-year capex and $6-9.5B in 2026 debt issuance creates balance sheet risk if rate recovery lags investment or if data center demand growth moderates [1][5]. The $11.4B offshore wind project has only $123M in remaining contingency (6% of remaining costs), leaving minimal buffer for final construction overruns [1].
key_catalysts: NextEra merger completion (expected 2027-2028) creating the nation's largest regulated utility with $2.25B in customer bill credits and combined scale for data center, renewable energy, and grid modernization investment [7]
Data center contracted capacity of 51 GW with 10.4 GW under executed Electric Service Agreements driving multi-year rate base growth, with commercial sales already surging 8.4% in Q1 2026 [1][3]

Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind project reaching completion at $11.4B budget with levelized cost of $84/MWh — well below the $149/MWh prudency cap — unlocking clean energy capacity and regulatory goodwill [1]
key_risks: NextEra merger regulatory risk — FERC, NRC, and three state utility commissions must approve; failure or extended delay could create prolonged uncertainty and limit Dominion's strategic flexibility as a standalone entity [7]

Broken dividend track record — 0 years of consecutive growth, -3.19% five-year CAGR, and 79.25% payout ratio make Dominion an impaired income holding that lags utility peers with established dividend growth streaks [5][6]

Capital intensity of $65B over five years combined with D/E of 155% and $6-9.5B in planned 2026 debt issuance creates balance sheet pressure if interest rates remain elevated or data center demand growth decelerates [1][5]
